narrative: As an Asian male applying to law school, your background includes a solid but mid-range academic start in Year 1, where you ranked in the 5th decile among your peers, followed by a dip to the 3rd decile by Year 3, alongside an undergraduate GPA of 2.80 and an LSAT score of 22—both below the typical averages of around 3.21 and 36.7 seen in students who pass the bar exam on their first try. Your family comes from a lower-middle income bracket, and you've indicated plans to attend part-time, which is less common as only about 7% of students choose that path compared to over 90% who go full-time. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first attempt based on profiles like yours, estimated a 48% chance that you would fail it. This probability tipped the scales toward rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to succeed right away to maintain their strong bar passage rates. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your LSAT score, which stood out as a major concern because it's notably lower than what the model typically sees in successful candidates. On a more positive note, your family income level worked in your favor, suggesting a resilience often linked to students from similar backgrounds who still pass the bar. Your undergraduate GPA also helped pull the prediction somewhat toward success, showing you have the foundational academic ability despite not being at the top end. Compared to typical students who get approved, your profile aligns more closely with those at higher risk of needing multiple bar attempts, especially given the LSAT's outsized role here. While other elements like your decile rankings and part-time plans played supporting roles, these top three factors—led by the LSAT—drove the overall decision. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, strengthening your Year 3 performance through additional coursework, and exploring full-time options if feasible, as these could significantly shift future predictions in your favor. Many students in your position have succeeded by targeting these areas, and reapplying with improvements often leads to acceptance elsewhere. Keep pushing, as your determination as an Asian male navigating these challenges already sets you apart.
